title: Dried Blood on Cotton
stainChemistry: Dried blood is fresh blood's chemistry plus oxidation. Hemoglobin has denatured and cross-linked with cellulose; iron has oxidized to iron oxide (the rust-brown part of the stain). The protein matrix is now insoluble in water and partially resistant to standard enzyme digestion. Heat at any point from drying onward sets the cross-links harder. Cotton tolerates enzymes, acid-side rinses, 3% H₂O₂, and hot wash — so the chemistry lane is wide. The issue is that dried blood responds incrementally: each cycle lifts another layer, and 2–3 cycles is the norm.
whyThisWorks: Cold soak reactivates the outer layer of denatured protein so enzyme can reach it. Liquid Protein Spotter (extended dwell) digests what it can reach; the rest waits for the next cycle. A dilute acid pass converts the oxidized iron to a soluble form. 3% H₂O₂ oxidizes residual iron-porphyrin chromophore. Repeat until shadow fades. Never hot-wash between cycles — sets what the enzyme hasn't cleared.
defaultAssumption: Expect 2–3 cycles. Fresh blood = easy single-pass; dried blood = patience. Any previous hot-water or hot-dryer exposure moves toward pro territory.

spottingProtocol.instruction: Submerge the stained area in cold water — soak 30–60 minutes. This rehydrates and softens the outer layer of denatured hemoglobin, letting the enzyme step reach protein that's been cross-linked.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Liquid Protein Spotter (PRENETT B / protease digestant) in warm water (30–38°C)
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply Protein Spotter directly OR submerge the stain in warm (30–38°C) enzyme solution. Dwell 30–60 min — longer than fresh blood, because dried protein breaks incrementally. Gently brush with a soft brush every 15 min. Enzyme denatures above 50°C; keep warm, not hot.

spottingProtocol.technique: Convert iron oxide to soluble form
spottingProtocol.equipment: Dilute acetic acid 5% aqueous OR lemon juice diluted 1:4
spottingProtocol.instruction: After the enzyme pass, a rust-brown shadow often remains — that's iron oxide from the hemoglobin. Dab dilute acid on the shadow. Converts the iron to a soluble form that rinses out. Rinse cool.
For residual pink / brown shadow, 3% H₂O₂ drop-by-drop. Dwell 5–10 min, blot. Skip on dark cotton.

spottingProtocol.technique: Second or third pass if needed
spottingProtocol.instruction: Dried blood rarely clears in one pass. If shadow remains, repeat steps 1–4. Each cycle lifts another layer. 2–3 cycles total is normal.

spottingProtocol.technique: Launder cold, dry only when clear
spottingProtocol.instruction: Wash COLD per care label — heat sets remaining blood. Air-dry or tumble LOW. Inspect BEFORE any heat drying. Only once the stain is fully gone can hot wash follow.

steps: 1. Cold water soak 30–60 min — rehydrate denatured protein.
2. Liquid Protein Spotter, extended 30–60 min dwell, warm (30–38°C).
3. Dilute acetic acid for oxidized iron shadow.
5. Repeat 1–4 for incremental clearing. 2–3 cycles normal.
6. Cold wash until stain is clear; only then a hot finishing wash.
products: Liquid Protein Spotter (PRENETT B or protease digestant)
professionalProtocol.temperature: cold soak; warm (30–38°C) enzyme; cool through acid + peroxide; cold wash until clear
warnings: Never use warm/hot water on the protein step — sets remaining blood
Dried blood is multi-cycle — set expectations
Never hot-wash or dry until stain is fully gone
Iron shadow is separate chemistry — don't skip acid step

homeSolutions.instruction: Dried blood is harder than fresh blood — not impossible, just slower. Expect 2–3 full cycles to fully clear it. This card assumes you know the stain is blood and it's been dried for a while (overnight +); if you just discovered a stain and don't know, look for a rust-brown / dark-brown ring — that's the iron oxidation that distinguishes dried blood from other brown stains.

homeSolutions.agent: Cold Water Soak — 30 Minutes to an Hour
homeSolutions.instruction: Submerge the stained area in cold water for 30–60 minutes. Cold only — warm or hot water COOKS remaining protein and makes the stain permanent. The soak rehydrates the outer layer of dried blood so the next step can reach it.

homeSolutions.agent: Enzyme Pretreat — Warm Water, Long Dwell
homeSolutions.instruction: Use an enzyme laundry pretreat (OxiClean Versatile, Biz, or Zout). Dissolve in WARM (not hot) water per the label. Apply to the stain, let it sit 30–60 MINUTES — longer than you would for fresh blood, because dried blood breaks down incrementally. Brush gently with a soft toothbrush every 15 min.

homeSolutions.agent: Lemon Juice or Vinegar for Rust-Brown Shadow
homeSolutions.instruction: After the enzyme pass, a rust-brown shadow often remains — that's the iron in the blood, oxidized. Dab a few drops of lemon juice or dilute white vinegar on the shadow, wait 5–10 minutes, rinse cool. The mild acid turns the iron into a soluble form you can rinse out.
For residual pink/brown on white cotton: dab 3% drugstore hydrogen peroxide, let it bubble 5–10 min, blot. Skip on dark cotton.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never mix peroxide with chlorine bleach or ammonia.

homeSolutions.agent: Repeat the Whole Cycle If Needed
homeSolutions.instruction: If a shadow remains, REPEAT steps 2–5. 2–3 full cycles is normal for dried blood. Don't be discouraged — each cycle lifts more.

homeSolutions.agent: COLD Wash Until the Stain Is Fully Gone
homeSolutions.instruction: After the shadow has faded, COLD WASH only. Air-dry. Do NOT run through a hot dryer or hot wash until the stain is 100% gone — heat permanently sets any remaining blood. Only once the fabric is fully clean can you hot-wash as a finishing step.
If the dried blood has already been through a hot wash or hot dryer before treatment, the protein has cooked into the fiber and home chemistry plateaus. Take to a dry cleaner.

steps: 1. Expect 2–3 cycles. Dried blood is patience, not power.
3. Enzyme pretreat in WARM water, 30–60 min dwell.
4. Lemon juice / vinegar dab for rust-brown iron shadow.
5. 3% hydrogen peroxide on residual shadow (whites only).
7. COLD WASH until stain is fully gone — then hot wash as finisher.
products: Enzyme laundry pretreat (OxiClean Versatile, Biz, or Zout)
Lemon juice or white vinegar (for iron tint)
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if dried blood has already been hot-washed or hot-dried (heat-set = pro only), or if 3 full home cycles plateau.
warnings: Cold water throughout until stain is fully gone
NEVER hot-wash or dry visible blood — permanent set
Enzymes die above 50°C — warm water, not hot
Multi-cycle is normal for dried blood
Never mix peroxide with chlorine bleach or ammonia

neverDo: Never hot-wash or dry dried blood while stain is visible — permanent
Never skip the iron-step — rust shadow is separate chemistry
fiberSensitivities: Cotton tolerates enzymes, acid, 3% H₂O₂, cold wash
Dyed cotton: skip or inside-seam-test peroxide
Heat-durable once protein is fully out — but only then

materialWarnings: Iron tint is a separate chemistry step

escalation.when: Dried blood has been hot-washed or hot-dried before treatment, or 3 full home cycles plateau.
escalation.whatToTell: "Dried blood on cotton, age [hours / days / older], home-treated with cold soak + enzyme pretreat + lemon juice + 3% H₂O₂. [Heat-set / no heat applied.] [N cycles completed.]"

contextQuestions: How long has the blood been dried?
Has the fabric been through any hot wash or dryer since?
Is there a visible rust-brown ring around the darker center? (iron fingerprint)
Is the cotton white, light, or dark/dyed?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Dried blood is the patient-removal version of blood chemistry. Multi-cycle framing + iron-step awareness are the protocol's two novel features vs fresh blood.
